\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Normal Form Conversion}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.LMN.NormalFormConversion
\section{Overview}

This module bridges the depth-2 Boolean circuit representation
(\lean{NAndCircuit} / \lean{NOrCircuit} built from literals \lean{BoolCircuit.Lit})
and the DNF/CNF normal forms used by the switching lemma. It defines the
conversion of circuit literals and clauses into switching-lemma terms, and proves
that these conversions preserve evaluation, term width, and the structural
hypotheses (no repeated indices, variable-injectivity) required downstream.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Declarations}

\begin{definition}[Convert a circuit literal to a switching-lemma literal]
\lean{BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, Literal}
Maps a Boolean-circuit literal $l : \lean{BoolCircuit.Lit}\,n$ to the corresponding
switching-lemma $\lean{Literal}\,n$, recording the same variable index and polarity.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Conversion preserves literal evaluation]
\lean{BoolCircuit.Lit.eval_eq_toLiteral_eval}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.Lit.eval, BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral, Literal.eval}
For every literal $l$ and input $x : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$, the circuit
evaluation $l.\mathrm{eval}\,x$ equals the evaluation of its converted literal
$l.\mathrm{toLiteral}.\mathrm{eval}\,x$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{definition}[AND-clause to a term]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit, Term}
Sends an AND-clause $\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{clause}\,lits$ to the
$\lean{Term}$ (a conjunction of literals) obtained by mapping each circuit literal
through \lean{Lit.toLiteral}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[OR-clause to a term]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral, Term}
Sends an OR-clause $\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{clause}\,lits$ to the $\lean{Term}$
obtained by mapping each circuit literal through \lean{Lit.toLiteral}; this term plays
the role of one CNF clause (a disjunction of literals).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Depth-2 OR-circuit to a DNF]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm, DNF}
Converts a depth-2 $\lean{NOrCircuit}$ (an OR of AND-clauses) into a $\lean{DNF}$, the
list of terms obtained by converting each AND-clause via \lean{NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm};
a bare clause maps to the empty DNF.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Depth-2 AND-circuit to a CNF]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm, CNF}
Converts a depth-2 $\lean{NAndCircuit}$ (an AND of OR-clauses) into a $\lean{CNF}$, the
list of clauses obtained by converting each OR-clause via \lean{NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm};
a bare clause maps to the empty CNF.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Conjunction of literals evaluates as a term]
\lean{BoolCircuit.foldr_and_lits_eq_term_eval}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.Lit.eval, BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral, Term.eval}
For any list of literals $lits$ and input $x$, the right fold of $\&\&$ over the literal
evaluations (starting from $\mathrm{true}$) equals the term evaluation
$\lean{Term.eval}\,(lits.\mathrm{map}\,\lean{Lit.toLiteral})\,x$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Disjunction of literals evaluates as a CNF clause]
\lean{BoolCircuit.foldr_or_lits_eq_clause_eval}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.Lit.eval, BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral, CNF.evalClause}
For any list of literals $lits$ and input $x$, the right fold of $||$ over the literal
evaluations (starting from $\mathrm{false}$) equals the CNF clause evaluation
$\lean{CNF.evalClause}\,(lits.\mathrm{map}\,\lean{Lit.toLiteral})\,x$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Depth-2 OR-circuit evaluates as its DNF]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.node_eval_eq_toDNF_eval}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.eval, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.eval, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF, BoolCircuit.foldr_and_lits_eq_term_eval, DNF.eval}
If every child $c$ of an $\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs$ is an AND-clause, then for
all $x$ the circuit evaluation $(\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs).\mathrm{eval}\,x$
equals the DNF evaluation $\lean{DNF.eval}\,((\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs).\mathrm{toDNF})\,x$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Depth-2 AND-circuit evaluates as its CNF]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.node_eval_eq_toCNF_eval}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.eval, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.eval, BoolCircuit.foldr_or_lits_eq_clause_eval, CNF.eval, CNF.evalClause, Term.eval}
If every child $c$ of an $\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs$ is an OR-clause, then for
all $x$ the circuit evaluation $(\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs).\mathrm{eval}\,x$
equals the CNF evaluation $\lean{CNF.eval}\,((\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs).\mathrm{toCNF})\,x$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Converted AND-clause has no repeated indices]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_nodup}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm}
If the variable indices of an AND-clause's literals are pairwise distinct, then the
converted term $(\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{clause}\,lits\,h).\mathrm{clauseToTerm}$ has the
\lean{Nodup} property.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Variable-injectivity of a converted AND-clause]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_var_inj}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.Lit.toLiteral, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm}
If the literal indices of an AND-clause are distinct, then within the converted term any
two literals sharing the same variable are equal.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Converted OR-clause has no repeated indices]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm_nodup}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_nodup, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm}
If the variable indices of an OR-clause's literals are pairwise distinct, then the
converted term $(\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{clause}\,lits\,h).\mathrm{clauseToTerm}$ has the
\lean{Nodup} property.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Variable-injectivity of a converted OR-clause]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm_var_inj}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_var_inj, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm}
If the literal indices of an OR-clause are distinct, then within the converted term any
two literals sharing the same variable are equal.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Width of a converted AND-clause]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_width}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm, Term.width}
The width of the term obtained from an AND-clause equals the number of its literals:
$\lean{Term.width}\,(\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{clause}\,lits\,h).\mathrm{clauseToTerm} = lits.\mathrm{length}$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Width of a converted OR-clause]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm_width}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm, Term.width}
The width of the term obtained from an OR-clause equals the number of its literals:
$\lean{Term.width}\,(\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{clause}\,lits\,h).\mathrm{clauseToTerm} = lits.\mathrm{length}$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Width bound for the DNF of a depth-2 OR-circuit]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF_width_bounded}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_width, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF, DNF.width}
If every AND-clause child has at most $w$ literals (with distinct indices), then the DNF
$(\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs).\mathrm{toDNF}$ has width at most $w$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Width bound for the CNF of a depth-2 AND-circuit]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF_width_bounded}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm_width, CNF.width}
If every OR-clause child has at most $w$ literals (with distinct indices), then the CNF
$(\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs).\mathrm{toCNF}$ has width at most $w$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[DNF terms of a depth-2 OR-circuit have no repeated indices]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF_terms_nodup}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_nodup, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF}
If every child of an $\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs$ is an AND-clause with distinct
literal indices, then every term in the resulting DNF has the \lean{Nodup} property.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Variable-injectivity of the DNF of a depth-2 OR-circuit]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF_var_inj}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.clauseToTerm_var_inj, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.toDNF}
If every child of an $\lean{NOrCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs$ is an AND-clause with distinct
literal indices, then in every term of the resulting DNF any two literals on the same
variable are equal.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[CNF clauses of a depth-2 AND-circuit have no repeated indices]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF_terms_nodup}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm_nodup}
If every child of an $\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs$ is an OR-clause with distinct
literal indices, then every clause in the resulting CNF has the \lean{Nodup} property.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Variable-injectivity of the CNF of a depth-2 AND-circuit]
\lean{BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF_var_inj}
\leanok
\uses{BoolCircuit.Lit, BoolCircuit.NAndCircuit.toCNF, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm, BoolCircuit.NOrCircuit.clauseToTerm_var_inj}
If every child of an $\lean{NAndCircuit}.\mathrm{node}\,cs$ is an OR-clause with distinct
literal indices, then in every clause of the resulting CNF any two literals on the same
variable are equal.
\end{theorem}
